meta.title: Tutorials · Aethyr docs
meta.description: Learn Aethyr by doing: short, story-driven tutorials covering how to read a Blueprint, author a mechanic by asking, refactor atomically, and recover from errors.

items.label: ② Say it, don't wire it
⑦ Grow a stat into a system
⑩ When a batch goes sideways

article.heading: Learn it by asking.
article.lead: Short stories, each a real thing you'd want to do: the exact sentence you'd say to your AI, and what Aethyr does under the hood. No tool memorization required, that's the whole point. Want one-line incantations instead? See the spellbook.

stories.heading: Read my Blueprint
stories.description: Understand an asset you've never seen.

stories.heading: Say it, don't wire it
stories.description: Turn a sentence into a compiled function.

stories.heading: All or nothing
stories.description: A multi-step refactor that can't half-apply.

stories.heading: Summon a pickup
stories.description: Build a whole actor from an empty Blueprint.

stories.heading: Onboard to a project
stories.description: Map a codebase you've never opened. Read-only.

stories.heading: Grow a stat into a system
stories.description: One bare float becomes a clamped, replicated system.

stories.heading: Fork a variant
stories.description: A new Blueprint from a proven one, no copy-paste.

stories.heading: When a batch goes sideways
stories.description: Read the failure, fix one op, re-run.

sections.heading: ① Read my Blueprint
sections.html:
You opened a project and there's a BP_Enemy nobody documented. Instead of clicking through every graph, ask.

youWhat does BP_Enemy do? Give me the gist, then the event graph.
Aethyr · your AI BP_Enemy (parent: Character), 5 variables, 2 functions. BeginPlay seeds health and starts a patrol Timeline; OnPerceptionUpdated switches to a chase state and calls FireWeapon. Here's the event graph topology…
used list_blueprints → summarize_blueprint → get_graph

Want a whole folder at once? Hand it to the audit agent:

youAudit /Game/AI: which Blueprints override BeginPlay, and are any variables unused?
bp-audit agent Scanned 23 Blueprints. 6 override BeginPlay; BP_Turret has 3 variables never read; BP_Spawner references a deleted actor class…
a read-only fan-out: nothing was modified

Reads can't change anything, so this is the safest way to start. No need to enable writes. Great for code review and onboarding.

sections.heading: ② Say it, don't wire it
sections.html:
You want a dash. You could place the nodes by hand… or describe it.

youGive the player a Dash: a function that adds a forward impulse, with a 2-second cooldown so it can't be spammed.
Aethyr · your AI Added bCanDash (bool) and DashCooldown (float = 2.0). Created a Dash function: guards on bCanDash, applies AddImpulse along the actor forward vector, then clears the flag and re-enables it on a 2s timer. Compiled clean.
used add_variable ×2 → add_function → add_node → wire_pins → set_pin_default → compile_function

Under the hood: with the bp-batches skill installed, your AI assembles the whole function as one unit and runs compile_function to confirm it builds, so you get a working node graph, not a pile of disconnected nodes.

sections.heading: ③ All or nothing
sections.html:
Renames and restructures are where things break. Make the AI do it as a single transaction that either fully lands or fully reverts.

youRename Health to CurrentHealth, add a MaxHealth float, and clamp damage against it, all as one atomic change. Preview it first.
Aethyr · your AI Here's the dry run: 1 rename, 1 new variable, 2 rewired nodes, 1 clamp inserted, no conflicts. Apply it?
used preview_ops (nothing written yet)
youApply.
Aethyr · your AI Applied as one batch, compiled, saved, backed up. If the clamp had failed to compile, the rename would have rolled back too, so your graph is never left half-done.
used apply_ops (atomic:true) → rolled-forward, backup written

Why preview? preview_ops is a read-only dry run: it tells you exactly what a batch will touch before a single byte is written. The bp-batches skill teaches the AI to always offer it for multi-step edits.

sections.heading: ④ When the ritual fails
sections.html:
Errors happen. With the bp-debug skill, they're legible instead of cryptic.

youThat returned AssetNotFound: /Game/AI/BP_Enemy.BP_Enemy. What went wrong?
bp-debug · your AI You passed an object path; Aethyr addresses assets by package path. Drop the .BP_Enemy suffix → /Game/AI/BP_Enemy. Re-running now… found it, 5 variables. (If a name's just close, the error lists the real ones so you can pick.)
no asset touched: failed calls are side-effect-free

Every error names what it tried, why it failed, and the closest valid value. A failed write makes no change, so you can always just fix the input and re-ask.

sections.heading: ⑤ Summon a pickup from nothing
sections.html:
No pickup asset on disk yet. By the end of this exchange you have one that heals on touch, compiled clean, just from describing it.

youI have nothing yet. Make a new Actor Blueprint at /Game/Items/BP_HealthPickup.
Aethyr · your AI Done. Created /Game/Items/BP_HealthPickup extending Actor, an empty shell ready to dress. It compiles (nothing in it to break yet).
used create_blueprint → get_compile_status
youGive it a body: a static mesh, and a sphere around it that acts as the pickup trigger. Make the sphere radius 96 and drop a basic sphere mesh on the mesh component.
Aethyr · your AI Added a StaticMeshComponent named Mesh and a SphereComponent named Trigger. Set Trigger's SphereRadius to 96 and pointed Mesh at the engine basic sphere, both on the component templates so every placed instance inherits them.
used add_component ×2 → set_component_property ×2
youAdd a heal amount I can tune per-instance in the editor, default 25.
Aethyr · your AI Added HealAmount (float), instance-editable, default 25.0. Designers can override it on each placed pickup without touching the Blueprint.
used add_variable
youNow the behavior: when something overlaps the trigger, heal it by HealAmount. Build the whole handler in one go and prove it compiles before you save.
bp-batches · your AI Let me stage it first so you can see the shape before anything touches disk. The handler binds the Trigger begin-overlap event, reads HealAmount, and calls a heal on the overlapping actor along with the wiring between them.
used compile_function (pseudocode → node graph) → preview_ops
youLooks right. Commit it.
bp-batches · your AI Applied as one unit. The begin-overlap handler fires on the Trigger, pulls HealAmount, and heals the overlapping actor by that value. Backed up the on-disk asset before the first save, compiled, and confirmed: 0 errors, 0 warnings. BP_HealthPickup now exists with a mesh, a trigger, a tunable heal, and a working graph.
used apply_ops → get_compile_status

Under the hood: the whole handler went in as one apply_ops batch wrapped in a single undo transaction. Either every node and wire lands together or none does, so the graph never sits half-built between calls.

Look before you leap: preview_ops renders the exact changes without writing a byte, and compile_function assembles the graph from plain pseudocode so you confirm it builds before it ever reaches the asset. Reads like get_compile_status are free to call as often as you like within a turn.

Safety net: every write passes a compile gate and copies the existing .uasset to a timestamped backup before the first save of the session. If a change goes wrong, restore the backup over the file while no editor holds it. Reads never mutate anything.

sections.heading: ⑥ Onboard to a project you've never opened
sections.html:
You just cloned a UE project you have never seen, so map its shape without clicking through a hundred assets and without touching a single byte.

youI just pulled this repo and I have never opened it. What am I even looking at? Don't change anything.
Aethyr · your AI Read the project file. It's MyGame, editor default map /Game/Maps/L_Startup, game default /Game/Maps/L_Arena. Enabled plugins of note: GameplayAbilities, ModularGameplay, CommonUI, and a project plugin MyGameCore. Nothing here writes, this is pure metadata off the .uproject.
used get_project_metadata
youGood. Show me the Blueprints, and the AI ones especially. Then tell me what the big ones actually do.
Aethyr · your AI Under /Game there are 47 Blueprints; /Game/AI holds 9, mostly BP_Enemy derivatives plus a BP_Turret. The three heaviest by graph size: BP_Player (parent ModularPlayerCharacter, 18 variables, an event graph that drives an ability-input loop), BP_Enemy (a perception-driven state machine that feeds a BehaviorTree), and BP_Turret (a fire-then-cooldown cycle on a timeline). Summaries are reflection reads, no asset is loaded for mutation.
used list_blueprints ×2 → summarize_blueprint ×3
youHand the whole /Game/AI folder to the auditor. I want to know which of these override BeginPlay, and whether anyone left dead variables lying around.
bp-audit agent Swept all 9. Five override BeginPlay (BP_Enemy and four of its children, each chaining to the parent). The turret does its setup in the construction script instead. Dead weight: BP_Enemy declares LastKnownTarget and bWasAlerted that are written but never read anywhere in its graphs, and BP_Turret has an unused SpinUpCurve. Everything I touched was a read, nothing was modified or saved.
used find_overriders → summarize_blueprint ×9 → list_variables ×9 → get_graph
youBefore I touch BP_Player, how much will move if I change it? Find everything that overrides its core input handler, and what depends on the character itself.
Aethyr · your AI HandleAbilityInput is overridden in 3 Blueprints down the player line. For blast radius: BP_Player is referenced by 12 assets, including /Game/Maps/L_Arena, a BP_GameMode that sets it as the default pawn, and two ability Blueprints that cast to it. That's your change surface, all from the reference graph, nothing opened for edit.
used find_overriders → get_referencers

Reads are free and fearless. Every call in this whole walkthrough is read-only, so you can map an unfamiliar project as aggressively as you like. Nothing here can dirty a package, trigger a save, or alter one node.

When you do start editing: the safety net is the same one the rest of the site shows. A ritual previews before it applies, every write passes a compile gate, and the original .uasset is backed up before the first save, so a bad edit rolls back to exactly what you cloned.

sections.heading: ⑦ Grow a bare stat into a system
sections.html:
A Blueprint holds one lonely Health float; you want a clamped, replicated system without ever leaving the asset half-rewritten.

youBefore we touch anything, what variables does BP_Enemy actually have right now?
Aethyr · your AI One variable: Health, a bare float (default 100), no category, not replicated. Nothing else. So it's a number with no rules around it yet.
used list_variables
youRight. Grow it into a real system: rename Health to CurrentHealth, add a MaxHealth float that's editable, replicated, default 100, and a TakeDamage(Amount) function that subtracts the damage and clamps CurrentHealth to the [0, MaxHealth] range. File both vars under a Vitals category. And do it as one move, I don't want a half-finished asset.
bp-batches · your AI Staged as a single batch, nothing written yet. Let me dry-run it first so you can see exactly what it touches.
used preview_ops
Aethyr · your AI The preview comes back clean: 1 rename (Health → CurrentHealth, all 0 existing references rewritten), 1 new variable (MaxHealth, float, EditAnywhere + replicated, default 100), 1 new function (TakeDamage with an Amount input, body subtracts then clamps to [0, MaxHealth]), and both vars filed under Vitals. No conflicts, no name collisions, no dangling pins.
used rename_variable · add_variable · add_function → add_function_input → add_node → wire_pins · set_variable_category ×2
youThat's exactly it. Commit it.
bp-batches · your AI Applied as one atomic transaction. The rename, the new MaxHealth, the TakeDamage function, and both category moves landed together. TakeDamage compiled clean, so the whole batch held; had the clamp failed to build, the rename would have reverted with it and you'd be back at the bare float, untouched. Verified after: BP_Enemy is compile-clean, no errors, no warnings.
used apply_ops → compile_function → get_compile_status

Why preview first: preview_ops walks the entire batch against the live Blueprint and reports what it would do, the rename count, the new symbols, any collision, without writing a single byte. You approve a plan, not a guess.

All or nothing: apply_ops runs the batch as one transaction behind the compile gate. If the TakeDamage clamp had failed to compile, the rename and the new variable roll back with it, so the asset is never stranded between a bare float and a finished system. A backup is written before the first save, either way.

Reads are free, so look first: list_variables never mutates and costs you nothing within a turn. Confirming the starting shape before you plan a change is the cheap habit that makes the expensive step safe.

sections.heading: ⑧ Fork a proven Blueprint into a variant
sections.html:
You have a working BP_Enemy and you want a meaner sibling, BP_EliteEnemy, that shares every line of logic but hits harder.

youMake a new Blueprint called BP_EliteEnemy with the same parent as BP_Enemy. I want a variant, not a fresh actor.
Aethyr · your AI Read BP_Enemy first: its parent is ACharacter. Created BP_EliteEnemy under /Game/AI/ on that same parent, so it inherits the identical component skeleton. Empty graphs for now. Compiled clean.
used read_blueprint → create_blueprint
youNow bring the brains across. Same functions as BP_Enemy, wired exactly the same. No hand-copying nodes.
Aethyr · your AI Listed the functions on BP_Enemy: TakeHit, ChooseTarget, and OnDeath. Declared matching stubs on the elite, then cloned each graph across whole, so every node, pin default, and wire came over intact, not redrawn. The event graph came too. Re-compiled the elite: clean, zero dangling pins.
used list_functions → add_function ×3 → clone_graph ×4 → compile_function
youGood. Now make it elite: 250 health instead of 100, and 40 damage instead of 25. Just the numbers.
Aethyr · your AI Touched only two defaults on the variant: MaxHealth 100 → 250 and AttackDamage 25 → 40. No graph edits, no structural change. Backed up the asset before the first write, then saved through the compile gate.
used set_variable_default ×2 → compile_function
youProve it. I want to see that the only differences from BP_Enemy are those two numbers, nothing structural snuck in.
Aethyr · your AI Ran a structural diff between the two Blueprints. Graphs, functions, components, and interfaces are identical, node for node and wire for wire. The diff reports exactly two deltas, both default-value: MaxHealth (100 vs 250) and AttackDamage (25 vs 40). Nothing structural drifted. Your variant is a true fork.
used bp_structural_diff

Why clone, not copy: clone_graph moves a graph as one unit, carrying every wire and pin default with it, so the elite's logic is the proven logic, not a re-drawing you have to re-verify. Hand copy-paste is where a missed connection hides.

The diff is your proof: bp_structural_diff is read-only and never touches either asset, so running it as the final step costs nothing and turns "I think they match" into "the only deltas are these two defaults." Reads are cheap within a turn, so verify freely.

If the elite had failed to compile after the stat change, the compile gate would have refused the save and the pre-write backup would still hold the last good copy, so a bad variant can never overwrite your working BP_Enemy or leave the elite half-written.

sections.heading: ⑨ A designer's balance pass, no editor needed
sections.html:
You suspect a weapon is overtuned, and you want to reason about it without opening the editor or touching a single asset.

youPull up WeaponStats as a table. I want to eyeball the damage column for anything that looks off.
Aethyr · your AI Read the WeaponStats DataTable. 11 rows. Damage clusters around 18 to 26 across the set, except one: Weapon_Rail sits at 72, roughly triple its neighbors. Same fire rate as the mid-tier rifles, so that is not a slow-but-heavy tradeoff. It reads like an outlier.
used read_data_table
youBefore I judge it, show me how it's meant to play. Read the ability set it belongs to, and tell me how its tag is categorized.
Aethyr · your AI The ability set grants one ability for that weapon, bound to the tag Ability.Weapon.Rail.Fire. Browsing the gameplay tags, it lives under Ability.Weapon alongside the other primaries, not under an Ability.Power or Ability.Ultimate branch. So the design files it as a standard primary fire, not a charged super. At 72 damage, the number and the category disagree.
used read_ability_set → list_gameplay_tags
youIf I nerf it, how far does that ripple? Find what references the Rail weapon asset.
Aethyr · your AI Read DA_Weapon_Rail for its details, then pulled its referencers. Four things point at it: the ability set you just saw, two loadout DataAssets, and one BP_Turret that mounts it as a default weapon. A damage change touches the player loadouts and that turret, but nothing in UI or VFX, so the blast radius is small and self-contained.
used read_data_asset → get_referencers
youOne more. Is this thing even enabled in the current mode? Check the config flag that gates it.
Aethyr · your AI Read bEnableExperimentalWeapons from the mode's config: it is True. So the Rail is live in this mode right now, which is why it surfaced in the balance feedback. You have the full picture: a primary-tier weapon at super-tier damage, enabled, referenced by two loadouts and one turret.
used read_config_value
youGood. When I do cut its damage, plan the change first so I can see what it touches before anything saves.
Aethyr · your AI Here is the dry run for the edit, nothing written: one value change to the Weapon_Rail row's damage, no rewired graph, no other rows affected. When you approve, the write goes through a compile gate and an automatic backup before it saves. Say the word and I apply it.
used preview_ops (dry run, nothing written)

Reads are cheap and safe. Every audit step here was a read: the table, the ability set, the tags, the referencers, the config flag. Nothing was loaded for write, nothing was checked out, and no .uasset changed on disk. You can trace a balance question end to end and walk away with the editor still untouched.

When you do decide to act, the same care holds. The plan above was a dry run that wrote nothing, and the real edit only lands after you approve it, passing a compile gate and writing an automatic backup before it saves. The audit you just did and the edit that follows are two deliberate, separate acts.

sections.heading: ⑩ When a batch goes sideways
sections.html:
A five-step edit trips on step four, and the whole thing rolls back untouched, exactly the safety net you want.

youBuild out BP_Turret: add a TargetActor variable, a FireRate float, an AcquireTarget function, then wire its found-actor pin into a new SetTargetActor call. Do it as one batch so it's all-or-nothing.
Aethyr · your AI Staged it as a single atomic apply_ops. Steps 1 through 3 applied, but step 4 failed the compile gate, so the whole batch was rejected. The error is pinned to that op: wiring FoundActor (Actor Object Reference) into the NewTarget input (Pawn Object Reference) is a type mismatch, the pins don't connect.
used apply_ops → get_compile_status
youSo nothing got written to the asset?
bp-debug · your AI Correct, nothing. Atomic means the whole batch lands or none of it does, and step 4 failed, so the save never happened. I re-read BP_Turret to confirm: no TargetActor, no FireRate, no AcquireTarget on disk. The Blueprint is byte-for-byte what it was before you asked. Reads like that are free inside the turn, so confirming costs nothing.
used read_blueprint → get_dirty_packages
youFix the one bad op, then check it before you commit anything.
Aethyr · your AI The fix is to cast first: I retargeted step 4 to feed AcquireTarget's output through a Cast To Pawn node, whose As Pawn pin (Pawn Object Reference) matches SetTargetActor's input. Then I dry-ran the whole five-step batch with preview_ops: it validates clean now, every pin type lines up, nothing touched the asset yet.
used preview_ops
youGood. Apply it for real.
Aethyr · your AI Done. apply_ops ran all five steps and saved. A backup of BP_Turret went to Saved/AethyrBackups/ before the first write, and the final get_compile_status came back clean, zero errors, zero warnings. The variables, the AcquireTarget function, and the cast-and-set wiring are all in.
used apply_ops → get_compile_status

Why this is safe: a batch apply_ops is atomic. Every op runs against an in-memory copy and the result must pass the compile gate before a single byte hits disk. One bad op fails the whole batch, and the on-disk asset stays exactly as it was, no half-written graph to clean up.

The loop that pays off: when a step fails, the error names the offending op and both pin types, so you fix one thing, not the batch. Re-run preview_ops to validate with zero writes, then apply_ops once it's green. The bp-debug skill walks this read, fix, re-preview loop, and reads stay cheap the whole way.
